name: 4-on-the-Floor
feel: Driving, relentless forward momentum. Classic house foundation.
examples: Interplanetary Criminal — Slow Burner (C4 kick, every beat)
analytical_note: The most fundamental house pattern. Every beat receives a kick, creating a metronomic pulse that locks the body into the groove. At 136 BPM the hits come fast enough to feel urgent. Use Drum Rack with a short, punchy kick sample.

name: 2-Step
pattern_description: Kick on beats 1 and 3 only. Snare/rim on 2 and 4. Syncopated hats fill the gaps.
feel: Skippy, bouncy, distinctly UK garage. Less relentless than 4-on-floor, more rhythmic space.
examples: Sammy Virji — Cops & Robbers (2-step kick with rim on 2+4)
Classic UK garage from the late 90s
analytical_note: The signature UK garage kick pattern. Only kicks on 1 and 3, leaving beats 2 and 4 to the snare/rim. This creates a 'lighter' feel than 4-on-floor and more room for syncopated hi-hat rolls. The open space between kicks is where the groove lives.

name: Off-Beat Hi-Hats
pattern_description: Closed hat on every off-beat (the 'and' of each beat): +1, +2, +3, +4
feel: Creates forward momentum and fills the space between kicks. Essential UK garage texture.
analytical_note: Off-beats land exactly halfway between beats. At 136 BPM there are 8 hat hits per bar. This pattern works against or with the kick to create drive. In IC Slow Burner, the hat never lands on a beat — only on the off-beats — making the kick hits feel heavier by contrast.

name: Velocity-Based Swing
pattern_description: Hats programmed on a rigid 16th-note grid but with cycling velocities to simulate human feel.
feel: Swing-like groove without using a groove pool. Subtle push-pull that prevents mechanical rigidity.
examples: BL3SS — Kisses (hat velocities cycle 93→110→127→113 across 16th-note grid)
analytical_note: Instead of using Ableton's groove pool or swing %, BL3SS programs hats at every 16th note but varies velocity in a repeating 4-note cycle. The louder notes (127) feel like downbeats, the quieter ones (93) recede. This creates perceived swing without actually shifting note timing.

name: Open Hat Accents
pattern_description: Open hi-hat hits placed on specific off-beats for texture and accent, usually beats 2.5 or 4.5.
feel: Adds air and openness. The sustained open hat ring creates space after a tight closed-hat run.
examples: Interplanetary Criminal — Slow Burner (open hat every off-beat in some sections)
analytical_note: Use choke groups in Drum Rack so the closed hat mutes the open hat on contact. The ring of the open hat decays into the next closed hat hit. Placing open hats on the off-beat of beat 2 or 4 creates a classic garage accent. Don't overuse — one or two per bar is enough.

name: Clap on 2 and 4
pattern_description: Clap or snare hits on beats 2 and 4 — the backbeat.
feel: Anchors the groove. The human instinct is to clap on 2 and 4, so this locks listeners in.
examples: MPH — Raw (UK Clap on D1, beats 2+4)
BL3SS — Kisses (Clap on A#3 — non-standard pitch, beats 2+4)
analytical_note: The backbeat is universal in electronic music. In garage, the clap sample choice matters more than placement — a tight, crispy UK clap sits differently to a room snare. BL3SS uses A#3 (not the standard C4 clap pitch) — experiment with pitch to shape the character of the hit.
